2026-06-30.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() to the Linux kernel's memory_hotplug module. The function takes an array of memory ranges and processes them as one operation under a single lock, ensuring atomicity and resolving a race condition between system daemons trying to manage the same resource. It first offlines every block in all ranges, then removes the ranges only if all are offline, reverting the whole operation if any offline fails. This provides callers with all-or-nothing semantics for the offline step, leaving the device in a consistent state even after a failed or interrupted unplug. The existing offline_and_remove_memory() function is updated to be a thin wrapper that passes its single range to the new helper.

2026-07-01.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() to the Linux kernel's memory_hotplug module. The function takes an array of memory ranges and processes them as one operation under a single lock, ensuring atomicity and resolving a race condition between system daemons trying to manage the same resource. It first offlines every block in all ranges, then removes the ranges only if all are offline, reverting the whole operation if any offline fails. This provides callers with all-or-nothing semantics for the offline step, leaving the device in a consistent state even in case of failure or interruption. The patch also updates the existing offline_and_remove_memory() function to be a thin wrapper that passes its single range to the new helper.

reviews.author: Richard Cheng
reviews.summary: Reviewer Richard Cheng noted that offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() should return 1 when a block is already offline, and suggested modifying the function to handle this case by setting rc = -EBUSY if it's greater than 0.
reviews.raw_body:
I think this can return 1, and it shouldn't.
device_offline() returns 1 when a block is already offline, and phase 1 passes that value through as-is.

Easy to hit with patch 0, offline one memory block via memoryN/state, then write
"unplugged" to daxX.Y/state. The store returns 1, userspace treats it as a partial write of 1 byte,
and retries the write with the rest of the string.

Maybe
"""
if (rc > 0)
    rc = -EBUSY;
"""

Best regards,
Richard Cheng.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a previously introduced race condition between memory hotplug and DAX/state, agreed to potentially reintroduce partial tests to address it.
reviews.raw_body:
Oh weird, I thought I'd solved this problem at some point.  I must have
botched a rebase or something.  Good catch.

I'd originaly had some tests that race memoryN/state and dax/state, but
I dropped them because it caused long testing cycles (mostly because i
was testing force unplugging).  Maybe I'll re-introduce some of these
partial tests and just avoid the destructive ones.

~Gregory

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that try_offline_memory_block() already clamps the value to 0 and plans to make it more obvious in a future revision.

2026-07-09.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() to the Linux kernel's memory_hotplug module. The function takes an array of memory ranges and processes them as one operation under a single lock, ensuring atomicity and resolving a race condition between system daemons trying to manage the same resource. It first offlines every block in all ranges, then removes the ranges only if all are offline, reverting the whole operation if any offline fails. This provides callers with all-or-nothing semantics for the offline step, leaving the device in a consistent state even in case of failure or interruption. The patch also updates the existing offline_and_remove_memory() function to be a thin wrapper that passes its single range to the new helper.
